Engineered Features\n\nThe base engineered features from earlier projects are recreated here to support advanced modeling.\n\nThese include:\n\n- log-transformed transaction amount\n- transaction hour and month\n- night transaction indicator\n- customer age\n- cyclical time encoding using sine and cosine transformations\n- customer-to-merchant geographic distance\n\nThese features provide a stronger representation of transaction behavior than the raw variables alone.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def haversine(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):\n    r = 6371.0\n\n    lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2 = map(radians, [lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2])\n\n    dlat = lat2 - lat1\n    dlon = lon2 - lon1\n\n    a = sin(dlat / 2) ** 2 + cos(lat1) * cos(lat2) * sin(dlon / 2) ** 2\n    c = 2 * atan2(sqrt(a), sqrt(1 - a))\n\n    return r * c","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.649357Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.649725Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.663852Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.649689Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.662926Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":127},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def base_feature_engineering(df):\n    df = df.copy()\n\n    df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"] = pd.to_datetime(df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"])\n    df[\"dob\"] = pd.to_datetime(df[\"dob\"], errors=\"coerce\")\n\n    df[\"trans_hour\"] = df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"].dt.hour\n    df[\"trans_day_num\"] = df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"].dt.dayofweek\n    df[\"trans_month\"] = df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"].dt.month\n    df[\"trans_date\"] = df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"].dt.date\n\n    df[\"log_amt\"] = np.log1p(df[\"amt\"])\n    df[\"age\"] = ((df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"] - df[\"dob\"]).dt.days / 365.25).astype(float)\n    df[\"is_night_transaction\"] = df[\"trans_hour\"].isin([22, 23, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]).astype(int)\n\n    df[\"hour_sin\"] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df[\"trans_hour\"] / 24)\n    df[\"hour_cos\"] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df[\"trans_hour\"] / 24)\n    df[\"month_sin\"] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df[\"trans_month\"] / 12)\n    df[\"month_cos\"] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df[\"trans_month\"] / 12)\n\n    df[\"customer_merchant_distance_km\"] = df.apply(\n        lambda row: haversine(\n            row[\"lat\"], row[\"long\"],\n            row[\"merch_lat\"], row[\"merch_long\"]\n        ),\n        axis=1\n    )\n\n    return df","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.664850Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.665278Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.681293Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.665248Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.680038Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":128},{"cell_type":"code","source":"train_df = base_feature_engineering(train_df)\ntest_df = base_feature_engineering(test_df)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.683409Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.683679Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.023153Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:10.683653Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.022038Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":129},{"cell_type":"code","source":"train_df[[\n the dataset.\n\n- Log transformation stabilizes the amount distribution\n- Time-based features capture transaction timing patterns\n- Age provides demographic context\n- Distance adds geographic fraud information\n\nTogether, these features create a stronger foundation for later aggregation, interaction, and optimization steps.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Leakage-Safe Aggregated Features\n\nLeakage-safe aggregation ensures that historical risk patterns are learned only from the training data and then applied to unseen test transactions.\n\n- Customer statistics capture normal spending and distance behavior without using future information\n- Merchant, category, and state fraud rates provide contextual risk signals learned from past observations\n- Age-band fraud rates add demographic context while preserving a realistic modeling setup\n\nThis approach is important because it prevents information from the test set from leaking into feature construction, making model evaluation more reliable and closer to real-world deployment.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def build_train_aggregates(train_df):\n    customer_stats = train_df.groupby(\"cc_num\").agg(\n        cust_avg_amt=(\"amt\", \"mean\"),\n        cust_median_amt=(\"amt\", \"median\"),\n        cust_std_amt=(\"amt\", \"std\"),\n        cust_txn_count=(\"amt\", \"count\"),\n        cust_avg_distance=(\"customer_merchant_distance_km\", \"mean\"),\n        cust_std_distance=(\"customer_merchant_distance_km\", \"std\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    merchant_stats = train_df.groupby(\"merchant\").agg(\n        merchant_fraud_rate=(\"is_fraud\", \"mean\"),\n        merchant_txn_count=(\"is_fraud\", \"count\"),\n        merchant_avg_amt=(\"amt\", \"mean\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    category_stats = train_df.groupby(\"category\").agg(\n        category_fraud_rate=(\"is_fraud\", \"mean\"),\n        category_txn_count=(\"is_fraud\", \"count\"),\n        category_avg_amt=(\"amt\", \"mean\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    state_stats = train_df.groupby(\"state\").agg(\n        state_fraud_rate=(\"is_fraud\", \"mean\"),\n        state_txn_count=(\"is_fraud\", \"count\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    age_band_map_df = train_df.copy()\n    age_band_map_df[\"age_band\"] = pd.cut(\n        age_band_map_df[\"age\"],\n        bins=[0, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 120],\n        labels=[\"Under 25\", \"25-34\", \"35-44\", \"45-54\", \"55-64\", \"65+\"]\n    )\n\n    age_band_stats = age_band_map_df.groupby(\"age_band\", observed=False).agg(\n        age_band_fraud_rate=(\"is_fraud\", \"mean\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    return {\n        \"customer_stats\": customer_stats,\n        \"merchant_stats\": merchant_stats,\n        \"category_stats\": category_stats,\n        \"state_stats\": state_stats,\n        \"age_band_stats\": age_band_stats\n    }","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.084626Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.085132Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.093715Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.085092Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.092760Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":131},{"cell_type":"code","source":"aggregates = build_train_aggregates(train_df)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.095223Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.095586Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.592948Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:39.095546Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.591885Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":132},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def apply_aggregates(df, aggregates):\n    df = df.copy()\n\n    df = df.merge(aggregates[\"customer_stats\"], on=\"cc_num\", how=\"left\")\n    df = df.merge(aggregates[\"merchant_stats\"], on=\"merchant\", how=\"left\")\n    df = df.merge(aggregates[\"category_stats\"], on=\"category\", how=\"left\")\n    df = df.merge(aggregates[\"state_stats\"], on=\"state\", how=\"left\")\n\n    df[\"age_band\"] = pd.cut(\n        df[\"age\"],\n        bins=[0, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 120],\n        labels=[\"Under 25\", \"25-34\", \"35-44\", \"45-54\", \"55-64\", \"65+\"]\n    )\n\n    df = df.merge(aggregates[\"age_band_stats\"], on=\"age_band\", how=\"left\")\n\n    return df","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.594335Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.594692Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.602776Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.594657Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.601069Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":133},{"cell_type":"code","source":"train_df = apply_aggregates(train_df, aggregates)\ntest_df = apply_aggregates(test_df, aggregates)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.604562Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.605049Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.085430Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:40.605008Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.084422Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":134},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Sequential Features\n\nSequential features capture the **order and timing of transactions**, which is critical in fraud detection.\n\n- `time_since_last_txn_sec` helps identify unusually rapid transactions, which may indicate fraud bursts or automated activity  \n- `prev_amt`, `amt_change_from_prev`, and `amt_ratio_to_prev` detect sudden spikes or drops in spending behavior  \n- Same-day features (`customer_txn_same_day`, `customer_amt_same_day`) reveal abnormal transaction intensity within a short time window  \n\nThese features shift the model from viewing transactions as isolated events to understanding them as part of a **behavioral sequence**.\n\n👉 This is powerful because fraud often occurs as **patterns over time**, not just single unusual transactions.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def add_sequential_features(df):\n    df = df.sort_values([\"cc_num\", \"trans_date_trans_time\"]).reset_index(drop=True)\n\n    df[\"prev_txn_time\"] = df.groupby(\"cc_num\")[\"trans_date_trans_time\"].shift(1)\n    df[\"time_since_last_txn_sec\"] = (\n        df[\"trans_date_trans_time\"] - df[\"prev_txn_time\"]\n    ).dt.total_seconds()\n\n    df[\"time_since_last_txn_sec\"] = df[\"time_since_last_txn_sec\"].fillna(-1)\n\n    df[\"prev_amt\"] = df.groupby(\"cc_num\")[\"amt\"].shift(1)\n    df[\"prev_amt\"] = df[\"prev_amt\"].fillna(df[\"amt\"])\n\n    df[\"amt_change_from_prev\"] = df[\"amt\"] - df[\"prev_amt\"]\n    df[\"amt_ratio_to_prev\"] = df[\"amt\"] / (df[\"prev_amt\"] + 1e-5)\n\n    daily_customer_txn = df.groupby([\"cc_num\", \"trans_date\"]).agg(\n        customer_txn_same_day=(\"amt\", \"count\"),\n        customer_amt_same_day=(\"amt\", \"sum\")\n    ).reset_index()\n\n    df = df.merge(daily_customer_txn, on=[\"cc_num\", \"trans_date\"], how=\"left\")\n\n    return df","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.086548Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.086792Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.094092Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.086769Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.093198Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":135},{"cell_type":"code","source":"train_df = add_sequential_features(train_df)\ntest_df = add_sequential_features(test_df)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.095130Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.095444Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.112776Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:46.095418Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.111903Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":136},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def add_deviation_and_interactions(df):\n    df = df.copy()\n\n    df[\"cust_std_amt\"] = df[\"cust_std_amt\"].fillna(0)\n    df[\"cust_std_distance\"] = df[\"cust_std_distance\"].fillna(0)\n\n    df[\"amt_vs_cust_mean\"] = df[\"amt\"] - df[\"cust_avg_amt\"]\n    df[\"amt_vs_cust_median\"] = df[\"amt\"] - df[\"cust_median_amt\"]\n    df[\"amt_to_cust_mean_ratio\"] = df[\"amt\"] / (df[\"cust_avg_amt\"] + 1e-5)\n    df[\"cust_zscore_amt\"] = (\n        (df[\"amt\"] - df[\"cust_avg_amt\"]) / (df[\"cust_std_amt\"] + 1e-5)\n    )\n\n    df[\"distance_vs_customer_avg\"] = (\n        df[\"customer_merchant_distance_km\"] - df[\"cust_avg_distance\"]\n    )\n    df[\"distance_zscore\"] = (\n        (df[\"customer_merchant_distance_km\"] - df[\"cust_avg_distance\"]) /\n        (df[\"cust_std_distance\"] + 1e-5)\n    )\n\n    df[\"night_amt\"] = df[\"is_night_transaction\"] * df[\"amt\"]\n    df[\"night_log_amt\"] = df[\"is_night_transaction\"] * df[\"log_amt\"]\n    df[\"merchant_risk_amt\"] = df[\"merchant_fraud_rate\"] * df[\"amt\"]\n    df[\"category_risk_amt\"] = df[\"category_fraud_rate\"] * df[\"amt\"]\n    df[\"night_merchant_risk\"] = df[\"is_night_transaction\"] * df[\"merchant_fraud_rate\"]\n    df[\"night_category_risk\"] = df[\"is_night_transaction\"] * df[\"category_fraud_rate\"]\n\n    return df","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.114048Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.114378Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.122701Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.114348Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:52.121441Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":137},{"cell_type":"code","source":"train_df SMOTE:\nis_fraud\n0    1289169\n1       7506\nName: count, dtype: int64\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":142},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### 1. Understanding the Imbalance\n\nBefore applying SMOTE, the dataset shows **extreme class imbalance**:\n\n- **Non-fraud (0):** 1,289,169  \n- **Fraud (1):** 7,506  \n\nThis means fraud cases make up **~0.58% of the data**, which is *highly skewed*.\n\nImplication:\n- A naive model could achieve **>99% accuracy** by predicting *“not fraud” every time*\n- But it would completely fail at detecting actual fraud","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)\nX_train_resampled, y_train_resampled = smote.fit_resample(X_train_scaled, y_train)\n\nprint(\"\\nAfter SMOTE:\")\nprint(pd.Series(y_train_resampled).value_counts())","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:56.641776Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:56.642046Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:17:58.694786Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:56.642014Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:17:58.693849Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"\nAfter SMOTE:\nis_fraud\n0    1289169\n1    1289169\nName: count, dtype: int64\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":143},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### 2. What SMOTE Does\n\nSMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique):\n\n- Generates **synthetic fraud examples**\n- Uses **feature-space interpolation** (not duplication)\n- Creates *new realistic fraud patterns* between existing fraud points\n\nAfter SMOTE:\n\n- **Non-fraud (0):** 1,289,169  \n- **Fraud (1):** 1,289,169  \n\nPerfectly balanced dataset\n\n---\n\n#### 3. Why This is Important\n\nWithout SMOTE:\n- Model becomes **biased toward majority class**\n- Fraud detection recall = **very low**\n- High **false negatives** (dangerous in real-world)\n\nWith SMOTE:\n- Model **learns fraud patterns better**\n- Improves:\n  - Recall (detecting fraud)\n  - F1-score\n- Enables model to **focus on minority behavior**","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Faster Optimization Strategy\n\nTo reduce runtime while preserving tuning quality, optimization is performed on a representative sample of the resampled training data.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"X_train_resampled_df = pd.DataFrame(X_train_resampled, columns=features)\ny_train_resampled_s = pd.Series(y_train_resampled, name=target_col)\n\nresampled_df = pd.concat([X_train_resampled_df, y_train_resampled_s], axis=1)\n\ntuning_sample_size = 120000\nif len(resampled_df) > tuning_sample_size:\n    tuning_df = resampled_df.sample(n=tuning_sample_size, random_state=42)\nelse:\n    tuning_df = resampled_df.copy()\n\nX_tune = tuning_df[features]\ny_tune = tuning_df[target_col]\n\nprint(\"Full resampled size:\", resampled_df.shape)\nprint(\"Tuning sample size:\", tuning_df.shape)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:17:58.696101Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:17:58.696493Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:18:00.185214Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:17:58.696455Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:18:00.184335Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"Full resampled size: (2578338, 45)\nTuning sample size: (120000, 45)\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":144},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Insight \n\nGiven the large size of the resampled dataset, optimizing models on the full training data would be computationally expensive and time-consuming. After applying SMOTE, the dataset grows significantly, reaching over 2.5 million samples. While this improves learning, it also introduces practical challenges in terms of training time and resource usage.\n\nTo address this, a **sampling-based optimization strategy** was implemented. Instead of performing hyperparameter tuning on the entire resampled dataset, a representative subset of 120,000 samples was selected for model tuning. This subset is large enough to capture the underlying data distribution, while being small enough to allow for efficient experimentation.\n\nBy doing this, the optimization process becomes significantly faster without sacrificing tuning quality. The model is still exposed to diverse patterns, including both normal and fraudulent transactions, but avoids the computational overhead of full-scale training during the tuning phase.\n\nOnce optimal hyperparameters are identified using this subset, they can then be applied to train the final model on the full dataset if needed. This two-step approach — **efficient tuning followed by full training** — is widely used in real-world machine learning pipelines.\n\nOverall, this strategy ensures a balance between **performance and efficiency**, allowing for rapid iteration while maintaining strong model generalization.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Random Forest Hyperparameter Tuning","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)\n\nrf_param_dist = {\n    \"n_estimators\": [100, 200, 300],\n    \"max_depth\": [8, 12, 16, None],\n    \"min_samples_split\": [2, 5],\n    \"min_samples_leaf\": [1, 2],\n    \"max_features\": [\"sqrt\", \"log2\"]\n}\n\nrf_search = RandomizedSearchCV(\n    estimator=rf,\n    param_distributions=rf_param_dist,\n    n_iter=6,\n    scoring=\"roc_auc\",\n    cv=2,\n    verbose=1,\n    random_state=42,\n    n_jobs=-1\n)\n\nrf_search.fit(X_tune, y_tune)\n\nbest_rf = rf_search.best_estimator_\n\nprint(\"Best Random Forest Parameters:\")\nprint(rf_search.best_params_)\nprint(\"\\nBest Random Forest CV ROC-AUC:\")\nprint(rf_search.best_score_)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:18:00.186388Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:18:00.186728Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.891118Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:18:00.186683Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.890117Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"Fitting 2 folds for each of 6 candidates, totalling 12 fits\nBest Random Forest Parameters:\n{'n_estimators': 200, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'min_samples_leaf': 1, 'max_features': 'sqrt', 'max_depth': None}\n\nBest Random Forest CV ROC-AUC:\n0.9999292519386653\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":145},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Insight \n\nThe tuning process was performed on the sampled dataset using **ROC-AUC as the evaluation metric**, which is particularly suitable for imbalanced classification problems. Unlike accuracy, ROC-AUC evaluates the model’s ability to distinguish between fraud and non-fraud across different thresholds.\n\nThe best-performing configuration achieved a **near-perfect ROC-AUC score (~0.9999)** during cross-validation. This indicates that the model is highly effective at ranking fraudulent transactions above legitimate ones.\n\nThe optimal parameters suggest:\n\n- A moderately sized forest (**200 trees**) provides sufficient learning capacity  \n- No restriction on tree depth (**max_depth=None**) allows the model to fully capture complex patterns  \n- Minimal constraints on splitting (**min_samples_split=2, min_samples_leaf=1**) enable detailed decision boundaries  \n- Using **sqrt feature selection** improves generalization by introducing randomness  \n\nOverall, the tuned Random Forest demonstrates strong capability in modeling the intricate relationships present in the engineered features. It effectively leverages temporal, behavioral, and contextual signals to distinguish fraudulent activity.\n\nHowever, while the ROC-AUC score is exceptionally high, it is important to validate performance on unseen test data and evaluate metrics such as **precision, recall, and F1-score**, especially given the real-world importance of minimizing missed fraud cases.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## XGBoost Hyperparameter Tuning\n\nA compact candidate search is used for XGBoost to make tuning faster while still exploring meaningful stratify=y_tune\n)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.892428Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.893079Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.993481Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.893046Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:10.992556Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":146},{"cell_type":"code","source":"xgb_param_candidates = []\n\nfor i, params in enumerate(xgb_param_candidates, start=1):\n    print(f\"Training XGBoost candidate {i}/{len(xgb_param_candidates)}\")\n    \n    model = XGBClassifier(\n eval_set=[(X_tune_val, y_tune_val)],\n        verbose=False\n    )\n    \n    val_probs = model.predict_proba(X_tune_val)[:, 1]\n    val_auc = roc_auc_score(y_tune_val, val_probs)\n    \n    xgb_results.append({\n        \"params\": params,\n        \"val_auc\": val_auc,\n        \"model\": model\n XGBoost Parameters:\n{'n_estimators': 250, 'max_depth': 4, 'learning_rate': 0.1, 'subsample': 0.9, 'colsample_bytree': 0.8, 'min_child_weight': 1, 'gamma': 0.1}\n\nBest XGBoost Validation ROC-AUC:\n0.9999959027775217\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":150},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Insight \n\nTo further improve model performance beyond the Random Forest baseline, an **XGBoost classifier** was introduced. XGBoost is a powerful gradient boosting algorithm known for its ability to capture complex, non-linear relationships while maintaining strong generalization performance.\n\nGiven the computational cost of exhaustive tuning, a **compact candidate search strategy** was adopted. Instead of exploring a large hyperparameter grid, a carefully selected set of parameter combinations was evaluated. This approach allows for efficient tuning while still covering meaningful variations in model complexity and learning behavior.\n\nThe tuning process involved splitting the tuning dataset into training and validation sets using **stratified sampling**, ensuring that the class distribution remained consistent across both sets. This is particularly important in fraud detection, where maintaining the proportion of rare fraud cases is critical for reliable evaluation.\n\nEach candidate configuration was trained and evaluated using **ROC-AUC**, which measures the model’s ability to distinguish between fraudulent and legitimate transactions across different classification thresholds. This metric is preferred over accuracy in imbalanced settings, as it focuses on ranking performance rather than raw prediction counts.\n\nThe results show that multiple configurations achieved **near-perfect validation ROC-AUC scores (~1.000)**, indicating that the model is highly effective at separating fraud from non-fraud in the feature space. The best-performing configuration included:\n\n- **250 trees**, providing sufficient model capacity  \n- A **moderate depth (max_depth = 4)** to balance complexity and generalization  \n- A **learning rate of 0.1**, allowing stable and efficient learning  \n- Subsampling (`subsample = 0.9`) and column sampling (`colsample_bytree = 0.8`) to introduce randomness and reduce overfitting  \n- Regularization parameters (`gamma`, `min_child_weight`) to control tree growth  \n\nThese settings suggest a well-balanced model that captures intricate fraud patterns while maintaining robustness.\n\nWhile the validation performance is extremely high, it is important to interpret these results with caution. Such near-perfect scores may indicate that the model has learned strong patterns from the engineered features, but it also highlights the need for **careful evaluation on unseen test data** to ensure that performance generalizes beyond the training environment.\n\nOverall, XGBoost demonstrates exceptional capability in leveraging the rich feature set, making it a strong candidate for the final fraud detection model.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Default Threshold Evaluation\n\nAfter training both models, their performance was evaluated on the test set using the default classification threshold of **0.5**. This threshold converts predicted probabilities into class labels, where transactions with a predicted fraud probability greater than or equal to 0.5 are classified as fraudulent.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test, model_name=\"Model\"):\n    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]\n    y_pred = (y_prob >= 0.5).astype(int)\n\n    print(f\"===== {model_name} =====\")\n    print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))\n    print(\"ROC-AUC:\", roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob))\n\n    return y_prob, y_pred","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.864861Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.865473Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.880234Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.865442Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.879190Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":151},{"cell_type":"code","source":"rf_prob_default, rf_pred_default = evaluate_model(best_rf, X_test_scaled, y_test, \"Optimized Random Forest\")","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.881574Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.881926Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:35.704491Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:30.881884Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:35.703611Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"===== Optimized Random Forest =====\n              precision    recall  f1-score   support\n\n           0       1.00      1.00      1.00    553574\n           1       0.55      0.96      0.70      2145\n\n    accuracy                           1.00    555719\n   macro avg       0.78      0.98      0.85    555719\nweighted avg       1.00      1.00      1.00    555719\n\nROC-AUC: 0.9980656391230225\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":152},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Insight \n\nThe optimized Random Forest model achieved an exceptionally high ROC-AUC score (~0.998), indicating strong overall ability to distinguish between fraudulent and legitimate transactions. \n\nHowever, when examining class-specific performance, a more nuanced picture emerges:\n\n- **Fraud Recall (0.96):** The model successfully identifies the majority of fraudulent transactions  \n- **Fraud Precision (0.55):** Nearly half of the flagged transactions are false positives  \n- **F1-Score (0.70):** Reflects the trade-off between recall and precision  \n\nThis suggests that while the model is highly effective at detecting fraud, it does so at the cost of generating a significant number of false alarms.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"xgb_prob_default, xgb_pred_default = evaluate_model(best_xgb, X_test_scaled, y_test, \"Optimized XGBoost\")","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:35.705546Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:35.705833Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:36.981337Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:35.705799Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:36.980293Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"===== Optimized XGBoost =====\n              precision    recall  f1-score   support\n\n           0       1.00      1.00      1.00    553574\n           1       0.72      0.96      0.82      2145\n\n    accuracy                           1.00    555719\n   macro avg       0.86      0.98      0.91    555719\nweighted avg       1.00      1.00      1.00    555719\n\nROC-AUC: 0.9987534409058902\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":153},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Insight\n\nThe optimized XGBoost model also demonstrates excellent performance, with a similarly high ROC-AUC score (~0.9987). However, it shows a notable improvement in fraud detection quality:\n\n- **Fraud Recall (0.96):** Maintains strong ability to capture fraudulent transactions  \n- **Fraud Precision (0.72):** Significantly reduces false positives compared to Random Forest  \n- **F1-Score (0.82):** Indicates a better balance between detection and accuracy  \n\nThis improvement highlights XGBoost’s ability to better leverage the engineered feature set, producing more reliable fraud predictions.\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\nAlthough both models achieve near-perfect ROC-AUC scores, this metric alone does not fully capture real-world performance. The difference becomes clear when examining precision and recall:\n\n- Random Forest is **more aggressive**, catching fraud but producing more false positives  \n- XGBoost is **more precise**, maintaining high recall while reducing unnecessary alerts  \n\nThis demonstrates an important principle in fraud detection:\n\n> A strong model is not just one that detects fraud, but one that does so efficiently without overwhelming the system with false alarms.\n\n---\n\n#### Why This Matters\n\nUsing a default threshold of 0.5 is a useful baseline, but it is rarely optimal in real-world fraud systems. The results clearly show that there is room to further improve performance by **adjusting the decision threshold** based on business priorities.\n\nFor example:\n\n- Lower thresholds → higher recall (catch more fraud)  \n- Higher thresholds → higher precision (reduce false alarms)  \n\nThis sets the stage for the next step: **threshold optimization**, where the model can be fine-tuned to achieve the ideal balance between fraud detection and operational efficiency.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## ROC Curve Comparison\n\nTo further evaluate model performance beyond a fixed threshold, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves were plotted for both the Random Forest and XGBoost models. The ROC curve illustrates the trade-off between the **True Positive Rate (Recall)** and the **False Positive Rate** across all possible classification thresholds.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))\n\nfor name, model in {\n    \"Random Forest\": best_rf,\n    \"XGBoost\": best_xgb\n}.items():\n    probs = model.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]\n    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, probs)\n    auc_score = roc_auc_score(y_test, probs)\n    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f\"{name} (AUC = {auc_score:.4f})\")\n\nplt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], \"r--\", label=\"Random Guess\")\nplt.xlabel(\"False Positive Rate\")\nplt.ylabel(\"True Positive Rate\")\nplt.title(\"ROC Curve Comparison — Project 4\")\nplt.legend()\nplt.show()","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:36.982448Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:36.982812Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:43.327365Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:36.982783Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:43.326335Z"}},"outputs":[{"output_type":"display_data","data":{"text/plain":"<Figure size 800x600 with 1 Axes>","image/png":"\n"},"metadata":{}}],"execution_count":154},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### Observations\n\nBoth models exhibit ROC curves that are tightly aligned with the **top-left corner** of the plot, indicating near-perfect classification capability. This is further supported by their extremely high AUC scores:\n\n- **Random Forest AUC ≈ 0.9981**\n- **XGBoost AUC ≈ 0.9988**\n\nThese values suggest that both models are highly effective at distinguishing between fraudulent and legitimate transactions, regardless of the threshold chosen.\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\nAlthough the ROC curves of both models appear nearly identical, XGBoost consistently achieves a slightly higher AUC score. This indicates a marginal but important improvement in its ability to rank fraudulent transactions higher than non-fraudulent ones.\n\nIn practical terms:\n\n- Both models are **excellent classifiers**\n- XGBoost demonstrates **superior ranking capability**\n- The difference, while small, becomes significant in high-volume systems where even minor improvements can translate into large operational gains\n\n---\n\n#### Why ROC Alone is Not Enough\n\nDespite the near-perfect ROC curves, it is important to recognize that ROC-AUC can sometimes be **misleading in highly imbalanced datasets**, such as fraud detection.\n\nBecause the number of legitimate transactions vastly outweighs fraudulent ones:\n\n- A model can achieve a high ROC-AUC even if it produces many false positives  \n- ROC does not directly reflect the **cost of errors** in real-world applications  \n\nThis reinforces an important takeaway:\n\n> High ROC-AUC does not guarantee optimal business performance.\n\n---\n\n#### Transition to Next Step\n\nThe ROC analysis confirms that both models are strong candidates. However, selecting the best model requires going beyond ranking performance and focusing on **operational effectiveness**.\n\nThe next step is to evaluate:\n\n- Precision vs Recall trade-offs  \n- Threshold optimization  \n- Business impact of false positives and false negatives  \n\nThis ensures that the final model is not only statistically strong, but also **practically deployable in a real-world fraud detection system**.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Precision-Recall Curve Comparison\n\nGiven the **severe class imbalance** in the dataset, Precision-Recall (PR) curves provide a more realistic evaluation of model performance than ROC curves. While ROC focuses on overall ranking ability, PR curves emphasize the model’s effectiveness in identifying the minority class — **fraudulent transactions**.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))\n\nfor name, model in {\n    \"Random Forest\": best_rf,\n    \"XGBoost\": best_xgb\n}.items():\n    probs = model.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]\n    precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, probs)\n    ap = average_precision_score(y_test, probs)\n    plt.plot(recall, precision, label=f\"{name} (AP = {ap:.4f})\")\n\nplt.xlabel(\"Recall\")\nplt.ylabel(\"Precision\")\nplt.title(\"Precision-Recall Curve Comparison — Project 4\")\nplt.legend()\nplt.show()","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:43.328574Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:43.328839Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.985560Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:43.328814Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.984370Z"}},"outputs":[{"output_type":"display_data","data":{"text/plain":"<Figure size 800x600 with 1 Axes>","image/png":"\n"},"metadata":{}}],"execution_count":155},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### Observations\n\nBoth models demonstrate strong performance, maintaining high precision across a wide range of recall values. However, a clear distinction emerges:\n\n- **Random Forest AP ≈ 0.9287**\n- **XGBoost AP ≈ 0.9644**\n\nXGBoost consistently maintains **higher precision at higher recall levels**, meaning it is better at detecting fraud **without excessively increasing false positives**.\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\nAs recall increases (capturing more fraud cases), precision typically declines due to an increase in false positives. However, XGBoost’s curve remains above Random Forest’s curve across most thresholds.\n\nThis indicates that:\n\n- XGBoost is more efficient at identifying fraudulent transactions  \n- It achieves **better balance between catching fraud and avoiding false alarms**  \n- It is more suitable for real-world deployment where both missed fraud and unnecessary alerts carry significant costs  \n\n---\n\n#### Business Interpretation\n\nIn a fraud detection system:\n\n- **High Recall** → Fewer fraudulent transactions go undetected  \n- **High Precision** → Fewer legitimate customers are incorrectly flagged  \n\nXGBoost’s superior PR performance suggests it can:\n\n- Catch more fraud cases  \n- While minimizing disruption to legitimate users  \n\nThis makes it a **more operationally effective model**, especially in high-volume financial systems.\n\n---\n\n#### Why This Matters More Than ROC\n\nAlthough both models achieved near-perfect ROC-AUC scores, the Precision-Recall curve reveals a more practical reality:\n\n> Not all high-performing models are equally useful in imbalanced, real-world scenarios.\n\nPR analysis highlights that:\n\n- The difference between models becomes **visible and meaningful**\n- Model selection should prioritize **precision-recall trade-offs**, not just ROC-AUC  \n\n---\n\n#### Conclusion\n\nWhile both models perform exceptionally well, **XGBoost clearly outperforms Random Forest in terms of precision-recall balance**.\n\nThis positions XGBoost as the **preferred model for fraud detection**, where the cost of false positives and false negatives must be carefully managed.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Threshold Tuning\n\nThe default threshold of 0.5 is not always optimal in fraud detection. Threshold tuning helps balance:\n- fraud recall\n- false positives\n- F1-score","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def threshold_metrics(y_true, y_prob, thresholds=np.arange(0.1, 0.91, 0.05)):\n    rows = []\n\n    for t in thresholds:\n        y_pred = (y_prob >= t).astype(int)\n\n        precision = precision_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)\n        recall = recall_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)\n        f1 = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0)\n        cm = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred)\n        tn, fp, fn, tp = cm.ravel()\n\n        rows.append({\n            \"threshold\": t,\n            \"precision\": precision,\n            \"recall\": recall,\n            \"f1_score\": f1,\n            \"true_negatives\": tn,\n            \"false_positives\": fp,\n            \"false_negatives\": fn,\n            \"true_positives\": tp\n        })\n\n    return pd.DataFrame(rows)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.986677Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.987254Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.997373Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.987106Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.996134Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":156},{"cell_type":"code","source":"rf_threshold_df xgb_prob_default)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.998664Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.999025Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.794217Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:49.998961Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.792944Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":157},{"cell_type":"code","source":"rf_threshold_df","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.795276Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.795573Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.809457Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.795542Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.808360Z"}},"outputs":[{"execution_count":158,"output_type":"execute_result","data":{"text/plain":" **low thresholds (0.1–0.3)**:\n  - Much higher precision than Random Forest  \n  - Fewer false positives for the same recall  \n\n- At **mid thresholds (~0.5–0.65)**:\n  - Strong balance between precision and recall  \n  - Example at 0.6:\n    - Precision ≈ 0.78  \n    - Recall ≈ 0.95  \n    - F1-score ≈ 0.85  \n\n- At **high thresholds (>0.8)**:\n  - Maintains high precision (>0.89)\n  - Retains better recall compared to Random Forest\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\nXGBoost dominates Random Forest across the entire threshold spectrum:\n\n- Higher precision at the same recall  \n- Lower false positives for equivalent fraud detection  \n- Better F1-scores at nearly all thresholds  \n\nThis confirms that:\n\n> XGBoost is not just a better model — it is a more controllable and reliable system for decision-making.\n\n---\n\n#### Optimal Threshold Selection\n\nThe choice of threshold depends on business priorities:\n\n- **Fraud prevention priority (maximize recall):**\n  - Use lower threshold (~0.3–0.5)  \n  - Accept more false positives to catch nearly all fraud  \n\n- **Customer experience priority (reduce false alarms):**\n  - Use higher threshold (~0.7–0.85)  \n  - Minimize disruption to legitimate users  \n\n- **Balanced strategy (recommended):**\n  - Threshold ≈ **0.6**\n  - Provides strong recall while maintaining acceptable precision  \n  - Best trade-off for most real-world fraud systems  \n\n---\n\n#### Business Interpretation\n\nThis analysis highlights a critical real-world insight:\n\n> The model itself is not the final decision-maker — the threshold is.\n\nBy tuning the threshold:\n\n- The same model can behave like a **strict fraud detector**  \n- Or like a **customer-friendly filter**  \n\nThis flexibility is essential in production systems where costs of errors are asymmetric.\n\n---\n\n#### Final Takeaway\n\nWhile both models perform well, **XGBoost combined with threshold tuning provides the most practical and deployable solution**.\n\nIt allows for:\n\n- Fine-grained control over fraud detection strategy  \n- Better balance between risk and customer experience  \n- Scalable deployment in high-volume transaction environments","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n# Threshold Performance Visualization\n\nTo better understand how model behavior changes across different thresholds, precision, recall, and F1-score were visualized for both Random Forest and XGBoost.\n\nThese plots provide an intuitive view of how each model balances fraud detection performance as the decision boundary shifts.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"def plot_threshold_metrics(df_thresholds, model_name):\n    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))\n    plt.plot(df_thresholds[\"threshold\"], df_thresholds[\"precision\"], marker=\"o\", label=\"Precision\")\n    plt.plot(df_thresholds[\"threshold\"], df_thresholds[\"recall\"], marker=\"o\", label=\"Recall\")\n    plt.plot(df_thresholds[\"threshold\"], df_thresholds[\"f1_score\"], marker=\"o\", label=\"F1-score\")\n    plt.xlabel(\"Threshold\")\n    plt.ylabel(\"Score\")\n    plt.title(f\"Threshold Tuning — {model_name}\")\n    plt.legend()\n    plt.grid(True)\n    plt.show()","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.834876Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.835238Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.854914Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.835206Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.853650Z"}},"outputs":[],"execution_count":160},{"cell_type":"code","source":"plot_threshold_metrics(rf_threshold_df, \"Random Forest\")","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.856239Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.856592Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:26:55.060667Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:54.856554Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:26:55.059829Z"}},"outputs":[{"output_type":"display_data","data":{"text/plain":"<Figure size 1000x600 with 1 Axes>","image/png":"\n"},"metadata":{}}],"execution_count":161},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### Random Forest Behavior\n\nThe Random Forest plot shows a clear trade-off pattern:\n\n- **Precision (blue line)** steadily increases as the threshold increases  \n- **Recall (orange line)** gradually decreases as the threshold increases  \n- **F1-score (green line)** peaks around **0.75–0.80**\n\nThis indicates that:\n\n- At low thresholds, the model aggressively flags fraud → high recall but many false positives  \n- At high thresholds, the model becomes conservative → high precision but misses more fraud  \n\nThe **optimal balance** for Random Forest appears near:\n\n> **Threshold ≈ 0.75**, where F1-score is maximized","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"plot_threshold_metrics(xgb_threshold_df, \"XGBoost\")","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:26:55.061673Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:26:55.061947Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.902511Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:26:55.061915Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.901364Z"}},"outputs":[{"output_type":"display_data","data":{"text/plain":"<Figure size 1000x600 with 1 Axes>","image/png":"\n"},"metadata":{}}],"execution_count":162},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### XGBoost Behavior\n\nThe XGBoost plot demonstrates a stronger and more stable performance:\n\n- Precision increases more rapidly and remains consistently higher than Random Forest  \n- Recall declines more slowly, indicating better fraud retention  \n- F1-score peaks later and higher, around **0.80–0.85**\n\nThis shows that:\n\n- XGBoost maintains **both high precision and high recall over a wider threshold range**  \n- The model is more **robust and less sensitive to threshold changes**\n\nThe optimal region for XGBoost is:\n\n> **Threshold ≈ 0.80–0.85**, where F1-score is highest\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\nComparing both plots reveals a critical difference:\n\n- Random Forest reaches its optimal performance earlier but declines faster  \n- XGBoost maintains strong performance across a broader range and achieves a higher peak  \n\nThis confirms that:\n\n> XGBoost provides a more stable and reliable decision boundary for fraud detection.\n\n---\n\n#### Practical Interpretation\n\nThese plots highlight an important real-world concept:\n\n- The **model outputs probabilities**  \n- The **threshold converts probabilities into decisions**\n\nBy adjusting the threshold, the same model can:\n\n- Act as a **strict fraud detector** (low threshold → high recall)  \n- Act as a **precision-focused filter** (high threshold → fewer false alarms)\n\n---\n\n#### Final Takeaway\n\nThe threshold performance plots reinforce previous findings:\n\n- XGBoost consistently outperforms Random Forest  \n- It offers **better control over the precision-recall trade-off**  \n- It achieves **higher peak performance (F1-score)**  \n\nThis makes XGBoost the **preferred model for deployment**, especially in systems where balancing fraud detection and customer experience is critical.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Best Threshold by F1-score\n\nTo determine the most effective decision boundary, the threshold that maximizes the F1-score was selected for each model. This ensures a balanced trade-off between precision (minimizing false positives) and recall (capturing fraud cases).","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"code","source":"best_rf_threshold_row = rf_threshold_df.loc[rf_threshold_df[\"f1_score\"].idxmax()]\nbest_xgb_threshold_row = xgb_threshold_df.loc[xgb_threshold_df[\"f1_score\"].idxmax()]\n\nprint(\"Best Random Forest Threshold by F1:\")\nprint(best_rf_threshold_row)\n\nprint(\"\\nBest XGBoost Threshold by F1:\")\nprint(best_xgb_threshold_row)","metadata":{"trusted":true,"execution":{"iopub.status.busy":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.904094Z","iopub.execute_input":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.904519Z","iopub.status.idle":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.911809Z","shell.execute_reply.started":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.904477Z","shell.execute_reply":"2026-03-27T13:27:00.910774Z"}},"outputs":[{"name":"stdout","text":"Best Random Forest Threshold by F1:\nthreshold               0.8000\nprecision               0.8730\nrecall                  0.8592\nf1_score                0.8661\ntrue_negatives    553,306.0000\nfalse_positives       268.0000\nfalse_negatives       302.0000\ntrue_positives      1,843.0000\nName: 14, dtype: float64\n\nBest XGBoost Threshold by F1:\nthreshold               0.9000\nprecision               0.9496\nrecall                  0.8965\nf1_score                0.9223\ntrue_negatives    553,472.0000\nfalse_positives       102.0000\nfalse_negatives       222.0000\ntrue_positives      1,923.0000\nName: 16, dtype: float64\n","output_type":"stream"}],"execution_count":163},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"#### Random Forest Optimal Threshold\n\nThe optimal threshold for Random Forest was found at:\n\n> **Threshold = 0.80**\n\nAt this point:\n\n- Precision: **0.8730**\n- Recall: **0.8592**\n- F1-score: **0.8661**\n\n**Interpretation:**\n\nAt this threshold, the model achieves a strong balance between detecting fraud and limiting false alarms. However, it still misses over 300 fraudulent transactions, indicating room for improvement in recall.\n\n---\n\n#### XGBoost Optimal Threshold\n\nThe optimal threshold for XGBoost was found at:\n\n> **Threshold = 0.90**\n\nAt this point:\n\n- Precision: **0.9496**\n- Recall: **0.8965**\n- F1-score: **0.9223**\n\n**Interpretation:**\n\nXGBoost achieves significantly better performance:\n\n- Higher overall separability between fraudulent and legitimate transactions.\n\nHowever, when focusing on fraud detection performance:\n\n- **XGBoost consistently outperforms Random Forest across all critical metrics**\n- It achieves **higher precision**, meaning fewer false alarms\n- It achieves **higher recall**, meaning more fraud is detected\n- It achieves a **higher F1-score**, indicating a better balance between precision and recall\n\n---\n\n#### Key Insight\n\n> **While both models are highly effective, XGBoost provides a more reliable and balanced fraud detection solution.**\n\nThe performance gap, although seemingly small numerically, translates into:\n\n- More fraud prevented  \n- Fewer legitimate transactions disrupted  \n- Improved trust in the system  \n\n---\n\n#### Engineering Perspective\n\nFrom a system design standpoint:\n\n- Random Forest offers strong baseline performance and interpretability  \n- XGBoost provides superior predictive power and robustness  \n\nThe improved performance of XGBoost can be attributed to:\n\n- Its ability to model complex, non-linear relationships  \n- Better handling of feature interactions  \n- Strong regularization mechanisms that reduce overfitting  \n\n---\n\n#### Final Recommendation\n\nBased on all evaluations:\n\n> **XGBoost (Threshold = 0.90) is selected as the final model for deployment.**\n\nIt delivers:\n\n- The highest fraud detection capability  \n- The lowest false positive rate  \n- The best overall balance (F1-score)  \n\n---\n\n#### Final Takeaway\n\nThis project demonstrates that:\n\n- Advanced feature engineering significantly enhances model performance  \n- Proper handling of class imbalance is critical in fraud detection  \n- Threshold tuning is essential for real-world optimization  \n- Model selection should consider both technical metrics and business impact  \n\n> **XGBoost emerges as a production-ready solution capable of delivering high accuracy, reliability, and operational efficiency in fraud detection systems.**","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"---\n## Feature Importance size 1000x800 with 1 Axes>","image/png":"\n"},"metadata":{}}],"execution_count":171},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"### Feature Importance Insights\n\nThe XGBoost model is primarily driven by transaction behavior features.\n\n- **customer_amt_same_day** is the most important feature, indicating that unusual daily spending patterns are a strong fraud signal  \n- **log_amt** and **amt** highlight that transaction size plays a key role  \n- Time-based features such as **is_night_transaction** and **night_amt** show that fraud is more likely during unusual hours  \n- Sequential features like **prev_amt** capture sudden changes in spending behavior  \n\n**Key Insight:**\n\n> Fraud detection is driven more by *behavioral patterns over time* rather than static customer attributes.\n\nThis confirms that engineered features capturing **spending patterns, timing, and deviations from normal behavior** are critical for model performance.","metadata":{}},{"cell_type":"markdown","source":"## Discussion\n\nThis project demonstrates how combining advanced feature engineering, class imbalance handling, and model optimization can significantly improve fraud detection performance.\n\nA key finding is that **behavioral features** — such as transaction patterns, spending deviations, and temporal signals — play a more critical role than static attributes. Features like *customer_amt_same_day*, *log_amt*, and time-based indicators consistently ranked among the most important, highlighting the importance of capturing dynamic user behavior.\n\nAdditionally, the results show that:\n- **SMOTE improves minority class learning**, enabling models to better detect fraud\n- **Threshold tuning is essential**, as default thresholds do not reflect real-world trade-offs\n- **XGBoost outperforms Random Forest**, particularly in balancing precision and recall\n\nOverall, the project moves beyond basic modeling and demonstrates a **production-aware approach** to fraud detection.\n\n---\n\n## Limitations\n\nDespite strong performance, several limitations should be considered:\n\n- **Potential overfitting risk**: Extremely high ROC-AUC scores (~0.999) may indicate that the model has learned dataset-specific patterns  \n- **Synthetic data bias (SMOTE)**: While SMOTE improves balance, it may introduce unrealistic patterns that do not fully reflect real fraud behavior  \n- **Lack of real-time evaluation**: The model was evaluated on static data, not in a live streaming or real-time detection environment  \n- **Feature leakage risk**: Although care was taken, aggregation features may still carry subtle leakage if not strictly time-aware  \n- **No cost-sensitive optimization**: The model does not explicitly account for financial costs of false positives vs false negatives  \n\n---\n\n## Next Steps\n\nTo further improve and move toward a production-ready system, the following steps are recommended:\n\n- **Implement time-based validation (e.g., rolling window validation)** to better simulate real-world deployment  \n- **Introduce cost-sensitive learning**, assigning higher penalties to missed fraud cases  \n- **Deploy as a real-time system** using streaming frameworks (e.g., Kafka + API model serving)  \n- **Perform model monitoring**, including drift detection and periodic retraining  \n- **Experiment with ensemble or stacking methods** to further boost performance  \n- **Optimize for explainability**, using tools like SHAP to interpret predictions  \n\n---\n\n## Final Conclusion\n\nThis project successfully developed a high-performance fraud detection pipeline by integrating:\n\n- Advanced feature engineering  \n- Class imbalance handling (SMOTE)  \n- Hyperparameter tuning  \n- Threshold optimization  \n\nAmong the evaluated models:\n\n> **XGBoost emerged as the best-performing model**, achieving the highest precision, recall, and F1-score while maintaining excellent ROC-AUC performance.\n\nFrom a practical perspective, this model:\n\n- Detects more fraudulent transactions  \n- Reduces false positives  \n- Provides a balanced and reliable decision framework  \n\n---\n\n### Final Takeaway\n\n> Fraud detection is not just a modeling problem — it is a **decision optimization problem**.\n\nBy incorporating threshold tuning and business-aware evaluation, this project demonstrates how machine learning can be effectively aligned with real-world operational goals.\n\n> **XGBoost (Threshold = 0.90) is recommended for deployment as a robust, efficient, and scalable fraud detection solution.**","metadata":{}}]}
